abstract: Deep research agents (DRAs) integrate LLM reasoning with external tools. Memory systems enable DRAs to leverage historical experiences, which are essential for efficient reasoning and autonomous evolution. Existing methods rely on retrieving similar trajectories from memory to aid reasoning, while suffering from key limitations of ineffective memory evolution and increasing storage and retrieval costs. To address these problems, we propose a novel Memory Intelligence Agent (MIA) framework, consisting of a Manager-Planner-Executor architecture. Memory Manager is a non-parametric memory system that can store compressed historical search trajectories. Planner is a parametric memory agent that can produce search plans for questions. Executor is another agent that can search and analyze information guided by the search plan. To build the MIA framework, we first adopt an alternating reinforcement learning paradigm to enhance cooperation between the Planner and the Executor. Furthermore, we enable the Planner to continuously evolve during test-time learning, with updates performed on-the-fly alongside inference without interrupting the reasoning process. Additionally, we establish a bidirectional conversion loop between parametric and non-parametric memories to achieve efficient memory evolution. Finally, we incorporate a reflection and an unsupervised judgment mechanisms to boost reasoning and self-evolution in the open world. Extensive experiments across eleven benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of MIA.

citations.fullCitation: Huichi Zhou, Yihang Chen, Siyuan Guo, Xue Yan, Kin Hei Lee, Zihan Wang, Ka Yiu Lee, Guchun Zhang, Kun Shao, Linyi Yang, et al. Memento: Fine-tuning llm agents without fine-tuning llms. arXiv preprint arXiv:2508.16153, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper is frequently cited as a state-of-the-art memory-based agent and is used as a primary baseline in the experiments. MIA's performance is directly compared against Memento, highlighting its relevance in demonstrating the proposed framework's superiority in abstracting memory into high-level guidance.
citations.title: Memento: Fine-tuning llm agents without fine-tuning llms

citations.fullCitation: Andrew Zhao, Daniel Huang, Quentin Xu, Matthieu Lin, Yong-Jin Liu, and Gao Huang. Expel: Llm agents are experiential learners. In Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, volume 38, pages 19632–19642, 2024.
citations.justification: The ExpeL framework is cited for its approach of learning from both successful and failed experiences. This is a core concept that MIA adopts and expands upon by retrieving both positive (successful trajectories) and negative (failed trajectories) paradigms to construct a rich contextual prior for planning.
citations.title: Expel: Llm agents are experiential learners

citations.fullCitation: Jinming Wu, Zihao Deng, Wei Li, Yiding Liu, Bo You, Bo Li, Zejun Ma, and Ziwei Liu. Mmsearch-r1: Incentivizing lmms to search. arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.20670, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper is cited as a foundational work that integrates multimodal search tools into agents, a key capability of the MIA's Executor. The main paper explicitly states that one of its baselines is built upon the MMSearch-R1 codebase, making it a direct technical foundation for the research.
citations.title: Mmsearch-r1: Incentivizing lmms to search

citations.fullCitation: Shunyu Yao, Jeffrey Zhao, Dian Yu, Nan Du, Izhak Shafran, Karthik Narasimhan, and Yuan Cao. React: Synergizing reasoning and acting in language models, 2023.
citations.justification: This paper introduces the ReAct paradigm, which is fundamental to the operation of MIA's Executor. The paper repeatedly mentions that the Executor interacts with its environment via a ReAct loop, demonstrating that this citation provides the core architectural principle for the agent's action and reasoning cycle.
citations.title: React: Synergizing reasoning and acting in language models

citations.fullCitation: Zhihong Shao, Peiyi Wang, Qihao Zhu, Runxin Xu, Junxiao Song, Xiao Bi, Haowei Zhang, Mingchuan Zhang, Y. K. Li, Y. Wu, and Daya Guo. Deepseekmath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models, 2024. https://arxiv.org/ abs/2402.03300.
citations.justification: This paper is the source of the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm, which is the specific reinforcement learning method used in MIA's two-stage alternating training strategy. This makes it a critical methodological citation as it provides the core optimization algorithm for training both the Planner and the Executor.
citations.title: Deepseekmath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: Memory Intelligence Agent (MIA)

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by Jingyang Qiao, Weicheng Meng, Yu Cheng, Zhihang Lin, Zhizhong Zhang, Xin Tan, Jingyu Gong, Kun Shao, and Yuan Xie. Their affiliations include East China Normal University, Shanghai Innovation Institute, Harbin Institute of Technology, Xiamen University, Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, and an Independent Researcher. Jingyang Qiao and Weicheng Meng are noted as having equal contributions. Zhizhong Zhang is the corresponding author, and Yuan Xie is the project leader.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Deep Research Agents (DRAs) represent an advancement in artificial intelligence, integrating the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) with external tools like search engines to accomplish complex, open-ended tasks. This typically involves a multi-round paradigm of interleaved reasoning and external information retrieval. As agents evolve towards long-horizon, multi-turn interactions, memory systems have become a critical component, enabling agents to accumulate experience, refine strategies, and improve performance over time rather than addressing each task in isolation.

Existing research in agent memory has primarily focused on long-context scenarios, where information is stored as traces of search experience. While these approaches have shown performance in various agentic applications, they exhibit limitations when applied to deep research agents. These limitations include attention dilution by long contexts, noise introduction from irrelevant content, substantial storage challenges for continuously operating agents, and increasing computational costs for retrieval over massive memory stores.

Furthermore, traditional long-context memory often captures knowledge-oriented or factual-oriented information (e.g., historical facts, retrieved documents), whereas deep research tasks often require process-oriented memory and conceptual knowledge that describe *how* a result is obtained (e.g., search trajectories, failed attempts, successful strategies). The objective of memory in deep research agents extends beyond merely storing retrieved knowledge; it aims to leverage historical experiences to guide future planning and exploration.

Prior memory systems that attempt to address these limitations frequently utilize pre-trained models as "planners" to generate Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompts for search path planning. However, these methods face several challenges: the Planner typically operates without task-specific training, leading to suboptimal planning; few-shot example selection relies solely on relevance, neglecting quality or frequency; and the Executor agent often struggles to adequately interpret and follow planning instructions without specific training. Consequently, the performance improvements from such memory systems have been limited.

The Memory Intelligence Agent (MIA) framework positions itself within this landscape by addressing these identified limitations. It aims to develop a memory mechanism that efficiently stores and evolves process-oriented memory, fosters effective collaboration between planning and execution agents, and enables continuous self-evolution, even in unsupervised environments. This work seeks to advance the field by proposing a more sophisticated and integrated approach to agent memory that moves beyond simple context expansion to enable deeper learning and adaptation.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop a novel Memory Intelligence Agent (MIA) framework that enhances the reasoning performance and self-evolution capabilities of Deep Research Agents (DRAs) by integrating brain-inspired memory mechanisms. This overarching goal is motivated by several specific challenges identified in existing DRA designs:

1.  **Ineffective Memory Evolution and Efficiency:** Current memory systems often struggle with managing the growth of stored experiences, leading to increasing storage and retrieval costs. The systems lack effective mechanisms for memory compression, distillation, and dynamic adaptation, which are crucial for maintaining efficiency and relevance over long periods of operation.
2.  **Limitations of Long-Context Memory:** Relying heavily on long-context memory in DRAs presents several problems:
    *   **Attention Dilution:** Large contexts can hinder an LLM's ability to focus on the immediate problem.
    *   **Noise Introduction:** Irrelevant or weakly related information in memory can degrade reasoning accuracy.
    *   **Storage and Retrieval Costs:** Continuously growing memory histories lead to significant storage overhead and increased computational expense for retrieval.
3.  **Lack of Process-Oriented Memory:** Existing memory systems tend to store factual knowledge rather than operational strategies or search trajectories, which are more critical for guiding future planning and exploration in deep research tasks. Agents need to learn *how* to solve problems, not just *what* the answers are.
4.  **Suboptimal Planning and Execution Collaboration:**
    *   **Untrained Planners:** Current methods often use pre-trained LLMs as planners without task-specific training, resulting in generic and suboptimal search plans.
    *   **Ineffective Example Selection:** Few-shot examples for CoT prompting are typically chosen based solely on relevance, overlooking crucial factors like quality and frequency.
    *   **Unprepared Executors:** The Executor agents are frequently not trained to interpret and follow the Planner's instructions effectively, leading to a disconnect between planning and execution.
5.  **Limited Autonomous Evolution in Open-World Scenarios:** Many memory systems rely on explicit supervision (ground-truth answers) for evaluation and updates. This limits their ability to continuously evolve and adapt in real-world, unsupervised environments where such supervision is often unavailable.

To address these motivations, MIA aims to introduce a framework that incorporates structured memory management, synergistic training between planning and execution modules, and robust self-evolution mechanisms that function even without direct supervision. The goal is to create agents that can internalize experiences, dynamically adapt their strategies, and operate autonomously with high efficiency and accuracy.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The Memory Intelligence Agent (MIA) framework is designed with a Manager-Planner-Executor architecture and operates through a planning-execution-memory loop, facilitating lifelong learning.

#### 4.1. Overall Architecture

*   **Planner:** This component acts as a parametric memory agent, responsible for generating search plans for given questions. It is initialized from a pre-trained LLM (e.g., Qwen3-8B).
*   **Executor:** This agent is tasked with interpreting and implementing the search plan step-by-step until a final result is obtained. It is initialized from a pre-trained Large Multimodal Model (LMM) (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL-7B).
*   **Memory Manager:** This is a non-parametric memory system comprising a memory buffer and a frozen pre-trained LLM (e.g., Qwen3-32B). Its role is to store compressed, high-value historical search trajectories that serve as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) cases, and to manage the buffer using context prompts.

The memory framework also incorporates two complementary mechanisms for lifelong learning: non-parametric memory for contrastive experience (retrieving high-value records) and parametric memory for long-term self-consolidation (distilling knowledge into the Planner's intrinsic capabilities).

#### 4.2. MIA Agent Loop

The agent loop for MIA consists of three main stages:

##### 4.2.1. Memory Retrieval

For each multimodal query, once sufficient historical experiences are accumulated, the Memory Manager performs retrieval. Visual inputs are converted into textual captions to align with the memory unit storage format. A hybrid retrieval strategy scores each memory unit across three dimensions:

*   **Semantic Similarity:** Measures the semantic distance between the current input (question and image caption) and historical questions/captions.
*   **Value Reward:** Prioritizes memory units with high historical success rates.
*   **Frequency Reward:** Rewards low-frequency memory units to encourage exploration of diverse knowledge.

Based on these scores, the system retrieves both successful trajectories (positive paradigms) and failed trajectories (negative constraints) to enrich the planning context.

##### 4.2.2. Collaborative Reasoning

The core reasoning process involves dynamic synergy between the Planner and Executor:

*   **Planner (Cognitive Hub):** Utilizes retrieved trajectories with a few-shot CoT strategy to decompose complex tasks into sub-goals and generate an initial search plan.
*   **Executor (Operational Terminal):** Interprets the plan and interacts with the environment using a ReAct loop. It performs external search tools and reasons over results to derive the final answer.

A dynamic feedback loop connects the two: the Executor reports its execution status to the Planner. If the Executor encounters an impasse or unexpected feedback, the Planner can trigger a Reflect-Replan mechanism (limited to once per task) to adjust the search plan.

##### 4.2.3. Experience Consolidation

An LLM Judger evaluates the final result. The Memory Manager then compresses the current search trajectories and updates memory units. Images are compressed into captions, and verbose trajectories are abstracted into structured workflow summaries (using a Qwen3-32B LLM). New memory is stored, or existing similar memory units are replaced based on high semantic similarity. Value rewards and frequency counts of relevant units are updated. A real-time exploration and update strategy retrains the Planner with the current batch of questions, trajectories, and results, internalizing episodic memory into parametric memory. After training, memory units are selectively cleared to prevent saturation.

#### 4.3. Two-Stage Alternating RL Training

MIA employs a two-stage alternating Reinforcement Learning (RL) strategy, based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), to enhance Planner-Executor cooperation:

##### 4.3.1. Executor RL Training

In Stage 1, the Executor (initialized from Qwen2.5-VL-7B) is trained to understand and follow plans, perform deep reasoning, and execute tool calls under the ReAct paradigm. The Planner is frozen and provides plans. The objective function (Eq. 1) includes a token loss masking operation. A rule-based composite reward function (Eq. 2) guides optimization, considering correctness (0.7 weight), successful tool calls (0.2 weight), and output format (0.1 weight).

##### 4.3.2. Planner RL Training

In Stage 2, with the Executor frozen, the Planner (initialized from Qwen3-8B) is trained to absorb memory contexts, plan for complex questions, and reflect on Executor feedback. The objective function (Eq. 3) incorporates memory context and the Executor. Its reward function (Eq. 4) considers the correctness of the final answer (0.7 weight), intermediate answer (0.2 weight), reflection effectiveness (0.05 weight), and output format (0.05 weight).

#### 4.4. Test-Time Learning (TTL)

MIA incorporates a novel online test-time learning mechanism, allowing continuous self-evolution during inference without interruption:

*   **Non-parametric Memory:** During exploration, the Planner generates multiple candidate plans. The Executor executes these, and an LLM Judger evaluates results, categorizing trajectories as successful or failed. The Memory Manager selectively extracts and compresses the shortest successful trajectory (positive paradigm) and one random failed trajectory (negative paradigm) into structured workflow summaries, which are stored as non-parametric memory.
*   **Parametric Memory:** Parameter updates are applied exclusively to the Planner. Rewards for each plan-trajectory pair are calculated, and advantages are computed. The Planner's parameters are updated via GRPO (Eq. 3), reinforcing successful strategies and penalizing flawed logic.
*   **Meta Plan Memory Strategy:** A Router (using the same pre-trained LLM as the Memory Manager) references examples in the Meta Plan Memory to select the optimal plan and trajectory from the generated rollouts as the final output, ensuring no label leakage during inference.

#### 4.5. Self-Evolution in Unsupervised Environments

To enable self-evolution without ground-truth answers, MIA proposes an unsupervised evaluation framework mimicking a scientific peer-review process (Figure 6). It uses three specialized LLM-based reviewers (Qwen3-32B) to assess different aspects of an agent's reasoning trajectory:
1.  **Reasoning & Logical Consistency:** Evaluates the logical progression and coherence.
2.  **Information Sourcing & Credibility:** Scrutinizes understanding of retrieved information and checks for factual hallucinations.
3.  **Result Validity:** Assesses the completeness and substantive nature of the final response.

An "Area Chair Agent" performs a meta-analysis of these structured reviews to make a definitive judgment (Correct/Incorrect). This mechanism provides an approximate supervision signal, enabling non-parametric memory selection and parametric memory updating, thus maintaining continuous self-evolution in open-world scenarios.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments conducted across eleven benchmarks demonstrate the performance of the MIA framework.

#### 5.1. Overall Performance on Multimodal Datasets

MIA achieves an average accuracy of 53.6 across multimodal datasets, outperforming the previous best memory-based method by an average of 5.46 percentage points. Specific improvements include a 3.3% increase on FVQA-test, a 6.4% increase on LiveVQA (a multi-hop task), and a 9.1% increase on the In-house 1 custom task. The research also notes that traditional contextual memory methods (e.g., RAG, Mem0, A-Mem) often underperform the "No Memory" baseline, indicating that long memory contexts can introduce noise and degrade performance. MIA, with its dual-memory mechanism and online parameter updating, reportedly bridges this gap effectively.

Furthermore, MIA, utilizing a 7B Executor (Qwen2.5-VL-7B), demonstrates performance comparable to or surpassing several larger closed-source general models such as GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro, and GPT-5.4, and is close to Gemini-3-Flash. It also maintains competitive and superior performance against specialized search agents like Deepeyes2, despite using a simpler toolset (basic text and image search). This is attributed to MIA's ability to leverage memory and internalize historical experiences, rather than relying on complex external tools.

#### 5.2. Overall Performance on Text-Only Datasets

MIA consistently outperforms alternative methods across text-only datasets, achieving an average accuracy of 53.5. It improves upon the Memento baseline by an average of 7.5 percentage points, with notable gains of 7.6% on 2Wiki and 8.8% on the GAIA benchmark. This suggests that MIA's Manager-Planner-Executor architecture and continuous evolution mechanisms are modality-agnostic and effective in complex text-only multi-hop reasoning tasks.

#### 5.3. Training Analysis

Analysis of reward curves during training indicates an overall upward trend for both the Planner and Executor, though with distinct patterns. The Planner's reward curve shows a clearer increase due to more direct feedback. The Executor's reward curve increases more slowly, attributed to indirect and less stable feedback. Response length analysis during Test-Time Learning (TTL) shows that MIA adapts to dataset-specific characteristics, with response lengths shifting towards patterns observed in 2Wiki (shorter) and LiveVQA (longer) datasets, indicating improved reasoning capabilities through reinforcement learning.

#### 5.4. Generalization to Closed-Source Executors

MIA exhibits consistent performance enhancements when integrated with powerful closed-source models (GPT-5.4, Gemini-3-Flash, and Claude-Sonnet-4.6) even without TTL. The magnitude of improvement is inversely correlated with the base model's capability: GPT-5.4 benefits most (+8.9% on LiveVQA, +6.4% on HotpotQA), followed by Gemini-3-Flash and Claude-Sonnet-4.6. This indicates MIA's generalizability and its ability to augment various LLMs.

#### 5.5. Tool Call Analysis

Tool call distribution analysis (Figure 9) suggests several conclusions:
*   **Memory Necessity:** Models lacking memory mechanisms (e.g., No-Memory, RAG) show low tool usage and poor accuracy, indicating memory is essential for multi-turn reasoning.
*   **Planning Effectiveness:** Methods with explicit planners (Memento, MIA) outperform long-context memory approaches (ReasoningBank, ExpeL), suggesting that planning for the current query is more effective than solely relying on historical experiences in a long context.
*   **Heterogeneous Memory + Continual Learning:** MIA, by combining multiple memory components with test-time learning, achieves the highest performance among memory-based systems.

#### 5.6. Ablation Study

An ablation study systematically evaluates the contribution of each MIA component. Each proposed module reportedly enhances reasoning accuracy across both multimodal and text-only tasks.
*   The Manager-Planner-Executor architecture, where memory guides the Planner, improves average multimodal accuracy by 3.5% and text-only by 4.15% over a baseline.
*   The reflection mechanism adds further gains (0.43% multimodal, 3.28% text-only).
*   The alternating RL training process for the Planner (using a smaller Qwen3-8B model) boosts overall performance by 2.37% (multimodal) and 1.72% (text-only).
*   The online TTL mechanism provides a substantial final push, increasing average accuracy by 3.23% (multimodal) and 2.64% (text-only).
In total, the MIA framework improves overall average accuracy by 8.94% on multimodal benchmarks and 12.38% on text-only benchmarks compared to the initial baseline.

#### 5.7. Unsupervised Self-Evolution

The unsupervised MIA achieves performance comparable to supervised baselines on multimodal tasks and surpasses almost all supervised baselines on text-only tasks, indicating the effectiveness of its unsupervised evaluation framework. Self-evolution experiments show consistent performance growth across multiple epochs during TTL (e.g., from 59.6% to 61.7% on multimodal tasks for epoch-1 to epoch-3), demonstrating MIA's ability to accumulate useful experience and solve previously failed problems without explicit supervision.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The Memory Intelligence Agent (MIA) framework represents an advancement in the development of Deep Research Agents (DRAs) by addressing several critical limitations of existing memory systems. Its significance stems from:

1.  **Enhanced Memory Management and Efficiency:** MIA proposes a Manager-Planner-Executor architecture that effectively decouples historical memory storage, parametric planning, and dynamic execution. By compressing verbose historical trajectories into structured workflows and distinguishing between non-parametric (explicit contextual reference) and parametric (internalized knowledge) memory, MIA mitigates the storage bottlenecks and noise interference associated with conventional long-context memory methods. This approach promises more efficient memory retrieval and reduced computational overhead.
2.  **Improved Planning and Execution Synergy:** The introduction of a two-stage alternating reinforcement learning (RL) paradigm for the Planner and Executor agents is crucial. This training strategy not only improves the Planner's ability to generate precise, context-aware plans and perform autonomous reflection but also significantly enhances the Executor's capacity to understand and follow these plans. This mutual alignment fosters more robust and reliable multi-turn reasoning.
3.  **Continuous Self-Evolution in Dynamic Environments:** The online test-time learning (TTL) mechanism is a key innovation, enabling the agent to continuously internalize environmental feedback into its model parameters while enriching its external memory base without interrupting ongoing exploration. Furthermore, the novel unsupervised evaluation framework, which mimics a scientific peer-review process, allows MIA to maintain continuous self-evolution even in the absence of explicit ground-truth labels. This is vital for deploying agents in real-world, open-ended environments where constant supervision is impractical.
4.  **Broad Applicability and Scalability:** The experimental results demonstrate MIA's ability to significantly enhance the performance of both frontier LLMs (e.g., GPT-5.4) and smaller, more lightweight models (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL-7B) across diverse multimodal and text-only deep research benchmarks. This indicates its broad applicability and scalability, offering a pathway to building powerful research agents with varying computational resources.
5.  **Foundation for Advanced AI Agents:** MIA's framework contributes to the foundation for developing more autonomous, robust, and lifelong learning AI agents. By demonstrating effective mechanisms for memory evolution, collaborative reasoning, and unsupervised adaptation, this work paves the way for future research into agents capable of tackling increasingly complex and dynamic tasks in fields requiring deep research and continuous learning.

In summary, MIA's contributions address fundamental challenges in agentic AI, offering a principled approach to integrating memory, planning, and execution that yields substantial performance improvements and enables robust self-evolution, with potential implications for a wide range of AI applications requiring intelligent information gathering and reasoning.
overview:
## Bridging Memory and Reasoning in Deep Research Agents

The advancement of Deep Research Agents (DRAs) has significantly enhanced the ability of artificial intelligence to perform complex, multi-turn reasoning tasks by integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) with external search tools. However, as these tasks grow in complexity and duration, traditional memory systems often struggle to maintain efficiency and relevance. Many existing agents store historical data as long-context search traces, which can lead to "attention dilution" where the model loses focus on the primary task, or the introduction of noise from irrelevant information. 

The Memory Intelligence Agent (MIA) framework addresses these limitations by introducing a structured, brain-inspired memory mechanism. Unlike systems that focus primarily on factual or knowledge-oriented memory (storing *what* was found), MIA emphasizes "process-oriented" memory—the strategies, search trajectories, and reasoning paths (the *how*) used to reach a result. By decoupling memory management, planning, and execution, MIA enables agents to learn from both successes and failures, evolving continuously even in environments where ground-truth answers are unavailable.

![MIA Agent Loop and Architecture](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04503v1/x2.png)
*Figure 1: The overarching MIA architecture featuring the planning-execution-memory loop across three stages: Memory Retrieval, Collaborative Reasoning, and Experience Consolidation.*

## A Manager-Planner-Executor Architecture

The MIA framework is built upon a tripartite architecture designed to separate high-level strategy from low-level execution, while maintaining a dedicated system for memory organization.

1.  **The Planner (Parametric Memory):** Serving as the cognitive hub, the Planner is an LLM trained to decompose complex multimodal queries into actionable search plans. It does not just act on the current query but utilizes retrieved historical experiences to generate a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) plan.
2.  **The Executor (Operational Terminal):** This component, often a Large Multimodal Model (LMM), is responsible for interpreting the Planner’s instructions. It interacts directly with the environment—performing web searches, analyzing images, and synthesizing results—using a ReAct (Reason + Act) paradigm.
3.  **The Memory Manager (Non-Parametric Memory):** The Manager acts as a non-parametric buffer that stores and organizes high-value search trajectories. It uses a frozen LLM to compress verbose, raw search traces into structured "workflow summaries," ensuring the memory remains efficient and easy to retrieve.

This division of labor ensures that the Planner can focus on "what to do next" based on past experience, while the Executor focuses on "how to perform the action" in the current environment.

## The MIA Agent Loop: From Retrieval to Consolidation

The operation of a MIA agent follows a continuous loop consisting of three distinct stages that facilitate lifelong learning.

### Stage 1: Memory Retrieval
When a new query arrives, the Memory Manager converts visual inputs into textual captions and performs a hybrid retrieval search. It looks for similar historical questions and their corresponding trajectories. The retrieval process is guided by three criteria:
- **Semantic Similarity:** How close the current question is to historical records.
- **Value Reward:** Favoring trajectories that previously led to successful outcomes.
- **Frequency Reward:** Encouraging the exploration of diverse knowledge by rewarding lower-frequency memory units.

Crucially, the system retrieves both "positive paradigms" (successful paths) and "negative constraints" (failed attempts), allowing the agent to avoid repeating past mistakes.

### Stage 2: Collaborative Reasoning
The Planner receives the retrieved trajectories and the current query to generate an initial search plan. The Executor then attempts to carry out this plan. A unique feature of MIA is the "Reflect-Replan" mechanism. If the Executor hits a dead end or receives unexpected feedback from a tool, it reports back to the Planner. The Planner then reflects on the failure and generates a revised, supplementary plan to steer the agent back on track.

### Stage 3: Experience Consolidation
Once a task is complete, an LLM-based Judger evaluates the output. Successful trajectories are compressed into structured workflows. Verbose interactions are abstracted into concise step-by-step summaries, such as: "1. Use visual search to identify the document $\to$ 2. Perform text search for specific keywords $\to$ 3. Cross-verify results." This prevents the memory buffer from becoming saturated with redundant or low-value data.

## Synergy through Two-Stage Reinforcement Learning

A common failure point in research agents is the disconnect between the planner and the executor; often, the planner generates plans the executor cannot follow, or the executor fails to provide useful feedback. MIA addresses this through a two-stage alternating Reinforcement Learning (RL) strategy using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).

In the first stage, the Executor is trained to follow plans and execute tool calls accurately while the Planner remains frozen. The reward function for the Executor, $R_{\text{Executor}}$, balances multiple objectives:

$$R_{\text{Executor}} = 0.7 \cdot r_{\text{correct}} + 0.2 \cdot r_{\text{tool}} + 0.1 \cdot r_{\text{format}}$$

Where $r_{\text{correct}}$ represents the accuracy of the final answer, $r_{\text{tool}}$ tracks successful tool usage, and $r_{\text{format}}$ ensures the output adheres to the required structure.

In the second stage, the Executor is frozen, and the Planner is trained to generate more effective plans and reflect on feedback. Its reward function, $R_{\text{Planner}}$, is slightly more complex:

$$R_{\text{Planner}} = 0.7 \cdot r_{\text{correct}} + 0.2 \cdot r_{\text{inter}} + 0.05 \cdot r_{\text{reflect}} + 0.05 \cdot r_{\text{format}}$$

Here, $r_{\text{inter}}$ rewards the quality of intermediate reasoning, and $r_{\text{reflect}}$ specifically incentivizes effective replanning after a failure. This alternating training ensures that both components are aligned and capable of sophisticated collaboration.

## Continuous Test-Time Learning (TTL)

One of the most innovative aspects of MIA is its ability to evolve during the inference phase without needing to stop for a separate training session. This is achieved through Test-Time Learning (TTL). 

![Test-Time Learning Framework](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04503v1/x3.png)
*Figure 2: The Test-Time Learning (TTL) mechanism, showing how successful trajectories are internalized into parametric memory while also updating the non-parametric memory bank.*

During inference, the Planner generates multiple candidate plans (rollouts). The Executor runs these plans, and the results are evaluated. Successful strategies are then used to update the Planner's parameters via an online GRPO update. This allows the agent to "internalize" successful workflows into its own weights (parametric memory) while simultaneously storing them in the external buffer (non-parametric memory).

## Self-Evolution in Unsupervised Environments

In real-world scenarios, agents often lack a "gold standard" or ground-truth answer to verify their findings. To enable self-evolution in these unsupervised settings, MIA introduces a framework modeled after a scientific peer-review process.

![Unsupervised Evaluation Framework](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04503v1/x4.png)
*Figure 3: The unsupervised self-evolution loop, where three specialized LLM reviewers provide structured feedback to an "Area Chair" agent to determine the validity of a reasoning trajectory.*

Three independent LLM reviewers assess different aspects of the reasoning process:
1.  **Reasoning & Logical Consistency:** Does the logic flow correctly from one step to the next?
2.  **Information Sourcing & Credibility:** Is the retrieved information understood correctly, or is the model hallucinating?
3.  **Result Validity:** Is the final answer complete and substantive?

An "Area Chair Agent" then synthesizes these reviews to make a final decision (Accept/Reject). If a trajectory is accepted, it is used as a high-quality supervision signal to update the agent's memory and parameters, allowing it to improve its performance autonomously.

## Performance and Tool Usage Analysis

MIA has been evaluated across eleven benchmarks, including multimodal datasets like LiveVQA and text-only benchmarks like GAIA and HotpotQA. It consistently outperformed traditional memory-based methods such as RAG and Mem0. On multimodal tasks, MIA achieved an average accuracy of 53.6, representing a significant gain over baseline "No Memory" models.

A key observation from the research is the relationship between memory and tool usage. Analysis of tool calls shows that models without structured memory tend to give up earlier or perform fewer search steps, resulting in lower accuracy. 

![Tool Call Analysis](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04503v1/x9.png)
*Figure 4: Distribution of tool calls across different memory methods. MIA shows a higher concentration of effective multi-turn tool interactions compared to simpler RAG or non-memory approaches.*

MIA's higher tool usage indicates a more persistent and thorough search strategy, enabled by its ability to plan and reflect. Furthermore, MIA's performance with a 7B-parameter Executor was found to be competitive with much larger closed-source models like GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Pro, highlighting the efficiency of its memory-driven approach.

## Significance and Future Impact

The MIA framework represents a shift in how AI agents manage experience. By moving from simple document-based memory to a dual-system process-oriented memory, MIA addresses the core challenges of long-term agent operation: noise, context limits, and the need for continuous adaptation.

The ability to evolve without human-provided labels through the peer-review evaluation mechanism opens the door for autonomous agents that can operate in niche research fields where data is scarce. Moreover, the synergy between the Planner and Executor, refined through alternating reinforcement learning, provides a template for building more reliable and communicative multi-agent systems. As agents move toward more complex, open-ended research tasks, the integration of structured memory and continuous learning will likely be a foundational requirement for truly autonomous intelligence.

keyInsights: Decoupling explicit historical memory (non-parametric) from internalized planning knowledge (parametric) effectively mitigates long-context issues and improves memory efficiency.
Synergistic training and dynamic feedback between planning and execution agents significantly enhance multi-turn reasoning and task completion reliability.
Continuous self-evolution in unsupervised environments is achievable through selective memory compression, parameter updates, and a robust LLM-based peer-review evaluation system.
originalProblem: Existing deep research agents struggle with attention dilution and noise from long-context memory, leading to inefficient retrieval and storage costs.
Traditional memory systems primarily store factual knowledge, lacking process-oriented memory crucial for guiding complex planning and exploration strategies.
Suboptimal collaboration between untrained planning and execution agents, compounded by ineffective few-shot example selection, limits performance in multi-turn reasoning.
results: MIA achieved an average accuracy of 53.6% on multimodal datasets, surpassing previous memory-based methods by an average of 5.46 percentage points, and maintained competitive performance against larger closed-source general models.
On text-only datasets, MIA attained an average accuracy of 53.5%, improving upon the Memento baseline by an average of 7.5 percentage points.

Ablation studies confirmed that each proposed module, including the Manager-Planner-Executor architecture, reflection mechanism, alternating RL training, and online TTL, individually contributes to improved reasoning accuracy, totaling an 8.94% increase on multimodal and 12.38% on text-only benchmarks.
solution: MIA introduces a Manager-Planner-Executor architecture that utilizes a non-parametric Memory Manager to store compressed, high-value historical search trajectories, and a parametric Planner to generate plans, with an Executor performing actions.
A two-stage alternating Reinforcement Learning (RL) strategy trains the Planner and Executor for synergistic cooperation, enhancing plan interpretation and execution.

An online Test-Time Learning (TTL) mechanism enables continuous self-evolution during inference, leveraging an unsupervised evaluation framework that mimics a scientific peer-review process to update memory and parameters without ground-truth labels.
summary.summary: The Memory Intelligence Agent (MIA) framework enhances deep research agents by integrating brain-inspired memory mechanisms, a Manager-Planner-Executor architecture, and a novel unsupervised self-evolution capability. It achieves an average accuracy of 53.6% on multimodal datasets and 53.5% on text-only datasets, outperforming previous memory-based methods by 5.46 and 7.5 percentage points, respectively.

title: Memory Intelligence Agent
